nativeRange.habitat: Cosmopolitan freshwater moss occurring along streams, brooks, riverbanks, lakes, ponds, and wet emergent surfaces; tolerates extreme conditions including acidic mining lakes and artificially lit caves

summary: A hardy cosmopolitan freshwater moss with slender, horizontally trailing shoots and a vivid, slightly transparent green; thrives across cold and tropical tanks, attaches to wood and rock as an epiphyte, and is widely used in nano scapes, breeding tanks, and shrimp setups.
careNotes: Leptodictyum riparium (Stringy Moss / Brook Moss) is one of the most adaptable aquarium mosses available, with a near-global natural distribution and tolerance for an exceptionally wide parameter range — temperatures from roughly 4 to 26°C (optimal ~10–22°C), pH 5–8, KH 2–21, and GH 0–30, making it equally suitable for coldwater, biotope, and tropical planted tanks. Lighting demands are low; it accepts low to medium-high illumination but performs best at low to medium intensities, where algae pressure on its fine foliage is minimized. CO2 is not required (the plant tolerates the full 5–40 mg/L range), though injection plus regular fertilization produces denser, more vigorous growth. The species grows as an epiphyte and should be attached to driftwood, rocks, or mesh with cyanoacrylate glue or cotton thread — do not bury it in substrate. Unlike upright mosses, L. riparium produces more or less horizontal shoots with flat, dense foliage and will readily form long, free-floating strands if left untethered, which can be used to advantage in breeding tanks and as shrimplet cover. Growth is medium and can be vigorous under good light and ferts, so regular trimming keeps the form compact. The plant is undemanding and highly forgiving — it survives acidic mining lakes (pH as low as 1.6 has been recorded) and artificially lit caves in the wild, and it is an excellent choice for beginners, low-tech tanks, shrimp/fry refuges, and emersed paludarium setups.
breedingNotes: Propagated very easily by division or cuttings: snip a portion of the moss mat and tie or glue it to new hardscape, where it will resume growth within days. Free-floating fragments will also continue growing and can be re-attached at any time. No special triggers are required; growth follows light, nutrient, and CO2 availability.

primary.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
primary.notes: Primary parameter source. Lighting low-to-high, CO2 5-40 mg/L (not required), temperature 4-26°C (optimal 10-22°C), pH 5-8, KH 2-21, growth medium, difficulty very easy, family Amblystegiaceae, native range cosmopolitan with Central European prevalence, epiphyte that readily forms floating strands.

additional.name: Aquasabi
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed temperature 4-26°C, pH 5-8, KH 2-21, GH 0-30, low-light tolerance, medium growth, very easy difficulty, epiphyte placement with horizontal shoots and flat dense foliage, CO2 not essential but beneficial, propagation by division/cutting, suitable for cold and tropical tanks.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Leptodictyum riparium
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Amblystegiaceae, Hypnales), common names (Kneiff's feathermoss, streamside Leptodictyum moss, stringy moss), cosmopolitan distribution (excluding Pacific Islands and Australia), maximum shoot length ~30 cm, ecological tolerances including very acidic waters.

additional.name: Keep Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed common names (Stringy moss, Brook moss), low-to-medium light, beginner difficulty, vigorous/rapid growth needing regular trimming, adherence to rocks and tree roots, popularity in nano aquariums and as breeding/fry cover, benefit from fertiliser.
